content.content:
1→

2→ 3→

4→ 5→

LogSeal

6→ 7→

Developer-first audit logging API for B2B SaaS companies.

8→ 9→## 🚀 Quick Start 10→ 11→### Prerequisites 12→ 13→- Node.js 20+ 14→- npm 10+ 15→- Docker & Docker Compose 16→ 17→### Setup 18→ 19→```bash 20→# Clone the repo 21→git clone https://github.com/your-org/logseal.git 22→cd logseal 23→ 24→# Install dependencies 25→npm install 26→ 27→# Start PostgreSQL and Redis 28→docker compose up -d 29→ 30→# Copy environment file 31→cp .env.example .env 32→ 33→# Push database schema 34→npm run db:push 35→ 36→# Seed the database (creates test account & API keys) 37→npm run tsx scripts/seed.ts 38→ 39→# Start all services in development mode 40→npm run dev 41→``` 42→ 43→### Project Structure 44→ 45→``` 46→logseal/ 47→├── apps/ 48→│ ├── api/ # Fastify REST API 49→│ ├── dashboard/ # TanStack Start dashboard (React 19) 50→│ └── worker/ # BullMQ background worker 51→├── packages/ 52→│ ├── database/ # Drizzle ORM schema & migrations 53→│ ├── sdk/ # @logseal/node SDK 54→│ ├── types/ # Shared TypeScript types 55→│ └── utils/ # Shared utilities 56→├── docker-compose.yml 57→└── turbo.json 58→``` 59→ 60→## 🛠️ Development 61→ 62→### Commands 63→ 64→```bash 65→# Start all services 66→npm run dev 67→ 68→# Build all packages 69→npm run build 70→ 71→# Type check 72→npm run typecheck 73→ 74→# Run database migrations 75→npm run db:migrate 76→ 77→# Open Drizzle Studio (database GUI) 78→npm run db:studio 79→``` 80→ 81→### Services 82→ 83→| Service | URL | Description | 84→|---------|-----|-------------| 85→| Dashboard | http://localhost:3000 | TanStack Start dashboard | 86→| API | http://localhost:3001 | REST API | 87→| Health | http://localhost:3001/health | Health check | 88→| PostgreSQL | localhost:5432 | Database | 89→| Redis | localhost:6379 | Queue & cache | 90→ 91→## 📚 API Reference 92→ 93→### Authentication 94→ 95→All API requests require a Bearer token: 96→ 97→```bash 98→curl https://api.logseal.io/v1/events \ 99→ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_test_..." 100→``` 101→ 102→### Emit an Event 103→ 104→```bash 105→curl -X POST https://api.logseal.io/v1/events \ 106→ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_test_..." \ 107→ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ 108→ -d '{ 109→ "action": "document.published", 110→ "organization_id": "org_demo", 111→ "actor": { 112→ "id": "user_123", 113→ "name": "Jane Smith", 114→ "email": "jane@example.com" 115→ }, 116→ "targets": [{ 117→ "type": "document", 118→ "id": "doc_456", 119→ "name": "Q3 Report" 120→ }], 121→ "metadata": { 122→ "previousStatus": "draft" 123→ } 124→ }' 125→``` 126→ 127→### Query Events 128→ 129→```bash 130→curl "https://api.logseal.io/v1/events?organization_id=org_demo&limit=10" \ 131→ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_test_..." 132→``` 133→ 134→## 📦 SDK Usage 135→ 136→```typescript 137→import { LogSeal } from '@logseal/node'; 138→ 139→const logseal = new LogSeal({ 140→ apiKey: 'sk_test_...', 141→}); 142→ 143→// Emit an event 144→await logseal.emit({ 145→ action: 'user.signed_in', 146→ organizationId: 'org_acme', 147→ actor: { id: 'user_123', email: 'jane@acme.com' }, 148→}); 149→ 150→// Query events 151→const events = await logseal.events.list({ 152→ organizationId: 'org_acme', 153→ action: 'user.signed_in', 154→}); 155→ 156→// Verify integrity 157→const result = await logseal.events.verify({ 158→ organizationId: 'org_acme', 159→}); 160→``` 161→ 162→## 🏗️ Architecture 163→ 164→``` 165→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 166→│ Customer App │ 167→│ └── @logseal/node SDK (batching, retry) │ 168→└───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘ 169→ │ HTTPS 170→ ▼ 171→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 172→│ LogSeal Platform │ 173→│ │ 174→│ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ 175→│ │ API Service │───▶│ Redis │───▶ Worker Service │ 176→│ │ (Fastify) │ │ (BullMQ) │ (hash chain, store) │ 177→│ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ │ 178→│ │ │ 179→│ └───────────────────▼ │ 180→│ PostgreSQL │ 181→│ (accounts, events, webhooks, etc.) │ 182→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ 183→``` 184→ 185→## 📄 License 186→ 187→MIT 188→

     2→  "name": "logseal",
     3→  "version": "0.0.1",
     4→  "private": true,
     5→  "description": "Developer-first audit logging API for B2B SaaS companies",
     6→  "workspaces": [
     7→    "apps/*",
     8→    "packages/*"
     9→  ],
    10→  "scripts": {
    11→    "build": "turbo run build",
    12→    "dev": "turbo run dev --concurrency=20",
    13→    "lint": "turbo run lint",
    14→    "test": "turbo run test",
    15→    "typecheck": "turbo run typecheck",
    16→    "clean": "turbo run clean && rm -rf node_modules",
    17→    "db:generate": "turbo run db:generate --filter=@logseal/database",
    18→    "db:migrate": "turbo run db:migrate --filter=@logseal/database",
    19→    "db:push": "turbo run db:push --filter=@logseal/database",
    20→    "db:studio": "turbo run db:studio --filter=@logseal/database",
    21→    "format": "prettier --write \"**/*.{ts,tsx,js,jsx,json,md}\""
    22→  },
    23→  "devDependencies": {
    24→    "@testcontainers/postgresql": "^11.11.0",

    35→    "vite-tsconfig-paths": "^6.0.5",
    36→    "vitest": "^4.0.18"
    37→  },

    41→  },

    43→    "node": ">=20.0.0"

content.content:
1→# LogSeal Deployment Guide
     2→
     3→Deploy LogSeal on Hetzner using Coolify for automated Docker deployments with TLS, monitoring, and backups.
     4→
     5→## Architecture Overview
     6→
     7→| Service    | Dockerfile               | Port | Description                |
     8→| ---------- | ------------------------ | ---- | -------------------------- |
     9→| API        | `apps/api/Dockerfile`    | 3001 | Fastify REST API           |
    10→| Worker     | `apps/worker/Dockerfile` | —    | BullMQ job processor       |
    11→| Dashboard  | `apps/dashboard/Dockerfile` | 3232 | TanStack Start SSR app  |
    12→| Docs       | `apps/docs/Dockerfile`   | 3002 | Next.js documentation site |
    13→| PostgreSQL | Coolify built-in         | 5432 | Primary database           |
    14→| Redis      | Coolify built-in         | 6379 | Queue & cache              |
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Part 1: Hetzner VPS
    19→
    20→1. Log in to [Hetzner Cloud Console](https://console.hetzner.cloud)
    21→2. Create a new server:
    22→   - **Location**: Nuremberg (or closest to your users)
    23→   - **Image**: Ubuntu 24.04
    24→   - **Type**: CCX23 (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM) — ~€25/mo
    25→   - **SSH Key**: Add your public key
    26→   - **Name**: `logseal-prod`
    27→3. Configure the **Hetzner Firewall** (create or assign):
    28→   - TCP 22 (SSH)
    29→   - TCP 80 (HTTP)
    30→   - TCP 443 (HTTPS)
    31→   - TCP 8000 (Coolify UI)
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→## Part 2: Install Coolify
    36→
    37→SSH into the server and run:
    38→
    39→```bash
    40→curl -fsSL https://cdn.coolify.io/install.sh | bash
    41→```
    42→
    43→After installation:
    44→
    45→1. Open `http://<server-ip>:8000` in your browser
    46→2. Create your admin account
    47→3. Go to **Servers** → add the current server as a "localhost" resource
    48→4. Verify Docker is running (Coolify checks this automatically)
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Part 3: Set Up Infrastructure Services
    53→
    54→### PostgreSQL
    55→
    56→1. Go to **Projects** → create "LogSeal"
    57→2. Click **+ New** → **Database** → **PostgreSQL 16**
    58→3. Configure:
    59→   - **Name**: `logseal-postgres`
    60→   - **Database**: `logseal`
    61→   - **User**: `logseal`
    62→   - **Password**: Generate a strong password
    63→4. Deploy and note the internal connection URL:
    64→   ```
    65→   postgresql://logseal:<password>@logseal-postgres:5432/logseal
    66→   ```
    67→
    68→### Redis
    69→
    70→1. Click **+ New** → **Database** → **Redis 7**
    71→2. Configure:
    72→   - **Name**: `logseal-redis`
    73→   - **Password**: Generate a strong password
    74→3. Deploy and note the internal connection URL:
    75→   ```
    76→   redis://default:<password>@logseal-redis:6379
    77→   ```
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→## Part 4: Deploy Application Services
    82→
    83→For each service, connect your GitHub repository and configure the Docker build.
    84→
    85→### API
    86→
    87→1. Click **+ New** → **Application** → connect your GitHub repo
    88→2. Configure:
    89→   - **Name**: `logseal-api`
    90→   - **Build Pack**: Dockerfile
    91→   - **Dockerfile Location**: `apps/api/Dockerfile`
    92→   - **Build Context**: `.` (monorepo root)
    93→   - **Ports Exposes**: `3001`
    94→   - **Health Check Path**: `/health`
    95→3. Add environment variables (see [Environment Variables](#environment-variables))
    96→4. Deploy
    97→
    98→### Worker
    99→
   100→1. Click **+ New** → **Application** → connect your GitHub repo
   101→2. Configure:
   102→   - **Name**: `logseal-worker`
   103→   - **Build Pack**: Dockerfile
   104→   - **Dockerfile Location**: `apps/worker/Dockerfile`
   105→   - **Build Context**: `.`
   106→   - **Ports Exposes**: Leave empty (no HTTP server)
   107→3. Add environment variables (same as API)
   108→4. Deploy
   109→
   110→### Dashboard
   111→
   112→1. Click **+ New** → **Application** → connect your GitHub repo
   113→2. Configure:
   114→   - **Name**: `logseal-dashboard`
   115→   - **Build Pack**: Dockerfile
   116→   - **Dockerfile Location**: `apps/dashboard/Dockerfile`
   117→   - **Build Context**: `.`
   118→   - **Ports Exposes**: `3232`
   119→3. Add build argument:
   120→   - `VITE_API_URL=https://api.logseal.io`
   121→4. Deploy
   122→
   123→### Docs
   124→
   125→1. Click **+ New** → **Application** → connect your GitHub repo
   126→2. Configure:
   127→   - **Name**: `logseal-docs`
   128→   - **Build Pack**: Dockerfile
   129→   - **Dockerfile Location**: `apps/docs/Dockerfile`
   130→   - **Build Context**: `.`
   131→   - **Ports Exposes**: `3002`
   132→3. Deploy
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## Environment Variables
   137→
   138→### API + Worker (shared)
   139→
   140→| Variable               | Description                    | Example                                                |
   141→| ---------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
   142→| `DATABASE_URL`         | PostgreSQL connection string   | `postgresql://logseal:pw@logseal-postgres:5432/logseal` |
   143→| `REDIS_URL`            | Redis connection string        | `redis://default:pw@logseal-redis:6379`                |
   144→| `NODE_ENV`             | Environment                    | `production`                                           |
   145→| `API_PORT`             | API listen port                | `3001`                                                 |
   146→| `API_HOST`             | API bind address               | `0.0.0.0`                                              |
   147→| `JWT_SECRET`           | Session signing secret         | Generate with `openssl rand -hex 32`                   |
   148→| `TOTP_ENCRYPTION_KEY`  | 2FA TOTP encryption key        | Generate with `openssl rand -hex 32`                   |
   149→| `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`    | Stripe API key                 | `sk_live_...`                                          |
   150→| `STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET`| Stripe webhook signing secret  | `whsec_...`                                            |
   151→| `RESEND_API_KEY`       | Resend email API key           | `re_...`                                               |
   152→| `DASHBOARD_URL`        | Dashboard URL (for emails)     | `https://app.logseal.io`                               |
   153→| `WORKER_CONCURRENCY`   | Worker job concurrency         | `10`                                                   |
   154→
   155→### Dashboard (build args)
   156→
   157→| Variable       | Description          | Example                      |
   158→| -------------- | -------------------- | ---------------------------- |
   159→| `VITE_API_URL` | API URL (build-time) | `https://api.logseal.io`     |
   160→
   161→### Docs
   162→
   163→| Variable   | Description | Example      |
   164→| ---------- | ----------- | ------------ |
   165→| `NODE_ENV` | Environment | `production` |
   166→
   167→---
   168→
   169→## Part 5: Domains & TLS
   170→
   171→In Coolify, configure domains for each service:
   172→
   173→| Domain              | Service   | Port |
   174→| ------------------- | --------- | ---- |
   175→| `api.logseal.io`    | API       | 3001 |
   176→| `app.logseal.io`    | Dashboard | 3232 |
   177→| `docs.logseal.io`   | Docs      | 3002 |
   178→
   179→For each service:
   180→
   181→1. Go to the service settings in Coolify
   182→2. Under **Domains**, add the domain
   183→3. Coolify automatically provisions Let's Encrypt TLS certificates
   184→
   185→### DNS Configuration
   186→
   187→Add these A records at your DNS provider:
   188→
   189→```
   190→api.logseal.io    A    <server-ip>
   191→app.logseal.io    A    <server-ip>
   192→docs.logseal.io   A    <server-ip>
   193→```
   194→
   195→---
   196→
   197→## Part 6: Database Migrations
   198→
   199→After the first deployment, run migrations via Coolify:
   200→
   201→1. Go to the **API** service in Coolify
   202→2. Click **Terminal** (or use **Execute Command**)
   203→3. Run:
   204→
   205→```bash
   206→node node_modules/.bin/drizzle-kit migrate --config packages/database/drizzle.config.ts
   207→```
   208→
   209→Alternatively, configure a **pre-deploy hook** in Coolify to run migrations automatically before each deployment.
   210→
   211→---
   212→
   213→## Part 7: Backups
   214→
   215→### PostgreSQL Backups
   216→
   217→1. Go to the PostgreSQL service in Coolify
   218→2. Navigate to **Backups**
   219→3. Configure:
   220→   - **Frequency**: Daily
   221→   - **Retention**: 7 days
   222→   - **Destination**: S3-compatible storage (Hetzner Object Storage works well)
   223→
   224→### Redis Persistence
   225→
   226→1. Go to the Redis service in Coolify
   227→2. Ensure AOF persistence is enabled in the Redis configuration
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Local Docker Testing
   232→
   233→Build and test images locally before deploying:
   234→
   235→```bash
   236→# Build each image from the monorepo root
   237→docker build -f apps/api/Dockerfile -t logseal-api .
   238→docker build -f apps/worker/Dockerfile -t logseal-worker .
   239→docker build -f apps/dashboard/Dockerfile --build-arg VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3001 -t logseal-dashboard .
   240→docker build -f apps/docs/Dockerfile -t logseal-docs .
   241→
   242→# Run with local infrastructure
   243→docker compose up -d  # starts PostgreSQL + Redis
   244→
   245→docker run --rm --network host \
   246→  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5433/traillog \
   247→  -e REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379 \
   248→  -e JWT_SECRET=dev-secret \
   249→  -e NODE_ENV=production \
   250→  logseal-api
   251→
   252→docker run --rm --network host \
   253→  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5433/traillog \
   254→  -e REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379 \
   255→  -e NODE_ENV=production \
   256→  logseal-worker
   257→
   258→docker run --rm -p 3232:3232 logseal-dashboard
   259→docker run --rm -p 3002:3002 logseal-docs
   260→```
   261→
   262→---
   263→
   264→## Troubleshooting
   265→
   266→### Build fails with argon2 error
   267→The API Dockerfile includes build tools (`python3`, `make`, `g++`) for argon2's native bindings. If you still see issues, ensure the `linux-headers` package is installed in the build stage.
   268→
   269→### Dashboard shows blank page
   270→Ensure `VITE_API_URL` was set as a build argument (not runtime env var). Vite inlines env vars at build time.
   271→
   272→### Next.js standalone output missing
   273→Verify `output: 'standalone'` is set in `apps/docs/next.config.mjs`. The Docs Dockerfile depends on this for a minimal production image.
   274→
   275→### Container can't reach PostgreSQL/Redis
   276→In Coolify, services on the same server communicate via Docker networks. Use the service name (e.g., `logseal-postgres`) as the hostname, not `localhost`.
   277→

content.content:
1→name: CI
     2→
     3→on:
     4→  pull_request:
     5→    branches: [main]
     6→  push:
     7→    branches: [main]
     8→
     9→jobs:
    10→  typecheck:
    11→    name: Typecheck
    12→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    13→    steps:
    14→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    15→      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    16→        with:
    17→          node-version: 20
    18→          cache: npm
    19→      - run: npm ci
    20→      - run: npx turbo typecheck
    21→
    22→  test:
    23→    name: Test
    24→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    25→    services:
    26→      postgres:
    27→        image: postgres:16
    28→        env:
    29→          POSTGRES_USER: test
    30→          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
    31→          POSTGRES_DB: logseal_test
    32→        ports:
    33→          - 5432:5432
    34→        options: >-
    35→          --health-cmd pg_isready
    36→          --health-interval 10s
    37→          --health-timeout 5s
    38→          --health-retries 5
    39→      redis:
    40→        image: redis:7
    41→        ports:
    42→          - 6379:6379
    43→        options: >-
    44→          --health-cmd "redis-cli ping"
    45→          --health-interval 10s
    46→          --health-timeout 5s
    47→          --health-retries 5
    48→    env:
    49→      DATABASE_URL: postgres://test:test@localhost:5432/logseal_test
    50→      REDIS_URL: redis://localhost:6379
    51→    steps:
    52→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    53→      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    54→        with:
    55→          node-version: 20
    56→          cache: npm
    57→      - run: npm ci
    58→      - run: npx turbo build --filter=@logseal/database --filter=@logseal/utils --filter=@logseal/types
    59→      - run: npm run db:migrate -w packages/database
    60→      - run: npm test -w apps/api
    61→
    62→  migration-safety:
    63→    name: Migration Safety Check
    64→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    65→    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    66→    steps:
    67→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    68→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    69→        with:
    70→          ref: ${{ github.base_ref }}
    71→          path: base
    72→      - name: Check new migrations for unsafe operations
    73→        run: |
    74→          NEW_MIGRATIONS=$(diff <(ls base/packages/database/src/migrations/*.sql 2>/dev/null | sort) <(ls packages/database/src/migrations/*.sql 2>/dev/null | sort) | grep '^>' | sed 's/^> //' || true)
    75→          if [ -n "$NEW_MIGRATIONS" ]; then
    76→            echo "::warning::New migration files detected. Please review for safety:"
    77→            echo "$NEW_MIGRATIONS"
    78→            for f in $NEW_MIGRATIONS; do
    79→              bash scripts/check-migration-safety.sh "$f"
    80→            done
    81→          else
    82→            echo "No new migrations in this PR."
    83→          fi
    84→
    85→  schema-drift:
    86→    name: Schema Drift Check
    87→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    88→    services:
    89→      postgres:
    90→        image: postgres:16
    91→        env:
    92→          POSTGRES_USER: drift
    93→          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: drift
    94→          POSTGRES_DB: drift_check
    95→        ports:
    96→          - 5432:5432
    97→        options: >-
    98→          --health-cmd pg_isready
    99→          --health-interval 10s
   100→          --health-timeout 5s
   101→          --health-retries 5
   102→    env:
   103→      DATABASE_URL: postgres://drift:drift@localhost:5432/drift_check
   104→    steps:
   105→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
   106→      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
   107→        with:
   108→          node-version: 20
   109→          cache: npm
   110→      - run: npm ci
   111→      - run: npx turbo build --filter=@logseal/database --filter=@logseal/utils --filter=@logseal/types
   112→
   113→      - name: Run migrations
   114→        run: npx tsx packages/database/src/migrate.ts
   115→
   116→      - name: Snapshot post-migration schema
   117→        run: |
   118→          pg_dump -h localhost -U drift -d drift_check --schema-only --no-owner --no-privileges \
   119→            | grep -v '^--' | grep -v '^$' | grep -v '^SET ' | grep -v '^SELECT ' | grep -v '^\\restrict' | grep -v '^\\unrestrict' \
   120→            | sort > /tmp/schema_before.sql
   121→        env:
   122→          PGPASSWORD: drift
   123→
   124→      - name: Apply Drizzle schema (db:push)
   125→        run: cd packages/database && npx drizzle-kit push --force
   126→        env:
   127→          DATABASE_URL: postgres://drift:drift@localhost:5432/drift_check
   128→
   129→      - name: Snapshot post-push schema
   130→        run: |
   131→          pg_dump -h localhost -U drift -d drift_check --schema-only --no-owner --no-privileges \
   132→            | grep -v '^--' | grep -v '^$' | grep -v '^SET ' | grep -v '^SELECT ' | grep -v '^\\restrict' | grep -v '^\\unrestrict' \
   133→            | sort > /tmp/schema_after.sql
   134→        env:
   135→          PGPASSWORD: drift
   136→
   137→      - name: Compare schemas
   138→        run: |
   139→          if diff -q /tmp/schema_before.sql /tmp/schema_after.sql >/dev/null 2>&1; then
   140→            echo "No schema drift. Migrations are in sync with the Drizzle schema."
   141→          else
   142→            echo "::error::Schema drift detected! The Drizzle schema has changes not covered by migrations."
   143→            echo ""
   144→            echo "db:push applied these changes that migrations missed:"
   145→            diff --unified /tmp/schema_before.sql /tmp/schema_after.sql || true
   146→            echo ""
   147→            echo "Fix: create a migration for the missing changes:"
   148→            echo "  cd packages/database && npm run db:generate"
   149→            exit 1
   150→          fi
   151→

1→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, bigint, integer, jsonb, inet, index } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     2→import { createEventId } from '@logseal/utils';
     3→
     4→/**
     5→ * The core audit events table
     6→ * This is append-only - no updates or deletes allowed
     7→ *
     8→ * Note: In production, you'd want to partition this table by received_at
     9→ * for better query performance. Drizzle doesn't support partitioning directly,
    10→ * so you'd create the partition structure via raw SQL migrations.
    11→ */
    12→export const auditEvents = pgTable(
    13→  'audit_events',
    14→  {
    15→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(createEventId),
    16→    accountId: text('account_id').notNull(),
    17→    organizationId: text('organization_id').notNull(),
    18→    environment: text('environment', { enum: ['test', 'live'] }).notNull(),
    19→
    20→    // Event content
    21→    action: text('action').notNull(),
    22→    actorType: text('actor_type').notNull().default('user'),
    23→    actorId: text('actor_id').notNull(),
    24→    actorName: text('actor_name'),
    25→    actorEmail: text('actor_email'),
    26→    actorMetadata: jsonb('actor_metadata').$type<Record<string, unknown>>().default({}),
    27→
    28→    // Targets - array of { type, id, name, metadata }
    29→    targets: jsonb('targets')
    30→      .$type<
    31→        Array<{
    32→          type: string;
    33→          id: string;
    34→          name?: string;
    35→          metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
    36→        }>
    37→      >()
    38→      .notNull()
    39→      .default([]),
    40→
    41→    // Arbitrary metadata
    42→    metadata: jsonb('metadata').$type<Record<string, unknown>>().default({}),
    43→
    44→    // Context
    45→    ipAddress: inet('ip_address'),
    46→    userAgent: text('user_agent'),
    47→    requestId: text('request_id'),
    48→
    49→    // Timing
    50→    occurredAt: timestamp('occurred_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull(),
    51→    receivedAt: timestamp('received_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    52→
    53→    // Integrity - hash chain
    54→    previousHash: text('previous_hash'),
    55→    eventHash: text('event_hash').notNull(),
    56→    sequenceNumber: bigint('sequence_number', { mode: 'number' }).notNull(),
    57→
    58→    // Idempotency
    59→    idempotencyKey: text('idempotency_key'),
    60→
    61→    // Skip-list hash chain (Phase 1)
    62→    skipHashes: jsonb('skip_hashes')
    63→      .$type<Array<{ offset: number; seq: number; hash: string }>>(),
    64→
    65→    // Merkle tree references (Phase 2)
    66→    merkleTreeId: text('merkle_tree_id'),
    67→    merkleLeafIndex: integer('merkle_leaf_index'),
    68→  },
    69→  (table) => [
    70→    // Query indexes
    71→    index('idx_events_org_time').on(table.organizationId, table.occurredAt),
    72→    index('idx_events_org_action').on(table.organizationId, table.action, table.occurredAt),
    73→    index('idx_events_org_actor').on(table.organizationId, table.actorId, table.occurredAt),
    74→    index('idx_events_account_env').on(table.accountId, table.environment, table.receivedAt),
    75→    index('idx_events_idempotency').on(table.accountId, table.idempotencyKey),
    76→    index('idx_events_org_seq').on(table.organizationId, table.sequenceNumber),
    77→  ]
    78→);
    79→
    80→export type AuditEvent = typeof auditEvents.$inferSelect;
    81→export type NewAuditEvent = typeof auditEvents.$inferInsert;
    82→

content.content:
1→---
     2→title: Getting Started
     3→description: LogSeal is an audit logging API for B2B SaaS. Ship tamper-proof audit logs to your customers in minutes, not months.
     4→---
     5→
     6→## What is LogSeal?
     7→
     8→LogSeal is an audit logging API purpose-built for B2B SaaS applications. Instead of building and maintaining your own audit log infrastructure, you integrate LogSeal with a few lines of code and get a production-ready audit trail for every tenant in your application.
     9→
    10→Every event you send is stored in a cryptographic hash chain, making your audit log tamper-proof and independently verifiable. Your customers can view their audit logs through an embeddable viewer, and you can query, export, and subscribe to events through the API.
    11→
    12→## Key Features
    13→
    14→**Tamper-proof hash chain** -- Every event is cryptographically chained to the previous event. If any event is modified, deleted, or reordered, the chain breaks. You and your customers can verify integrity at any time through the verification API.
    15→
    16→**Multi-tenant organizations** -- Events are scoped to organizations that map to your customers' tenants. Organizations are automatically created when you emit events, or you can create them explicitly. Test and live environments are fully separated.
    17→
    18→**Real-time webhooks** -- Subscribe to events like `event.created`, `export.completed`, and `export.failed`. Webhooks can be scoped globally or to a specific organization, with automatic retries on failure.
    19→
    20→**Embeddable viewer tokens** -- Generate short-lived, read-only tokens scoped to a single organization. Use these to embed an audit log viewer directly in your product without exposing your API key. Available on Pro and Business plans.
    21→
    22→**Batch ingestion** -- The SDK automatically batches events for efficient delivery. Use `emit()` for fire-and-forget batching or `emitSync()` when you need confirmation that an event was persisted.
    23→
    24→**Event schemas** -- Define schemas to validate event structure, enforce naming conventions, and document your audit log actions. Enable strict mode to reject events that don't match a defined schema.
    25→
    26→**CSV/JSON exports** -- Generate downloadable exports of audit events filtered by date range, action, or actor. Exports run asynchronously and provide a download URL when complete. Available on Pro and Business plans.
    27→
    28→## Next Steps
    29→
    30→<Cards>
    31→  <Card title="Quickstart" href="/docs/quickstart">
    32→    Install the SDK and emit your first event in under 5 minutes.
    33→  </Card>
    34→  <Card title="API Reference" href="/api-reference">
    35→    Full reference for all REST API endpoints.
    36→  </Card>
    37→  <Card title="Node.js SDK" href="/docs/sdk">
    38→    Detailed SDK documentation including configuration, batching, and error handling.
    39→  </Card>
    40→  <Card title="Authentication" href="/docs/authentication">
    41→    API key types, bearer tokens, and viewer tokens.
    42→  </Card>
    43→  <Card title="Events" href="/docs/events">
    44→    Event structure, naming conventions, and querying.
    45→  </Card>
    46→  <Card title="Organizations" href="/docs/organizations">
    47→    Multi-tenant model and organization management.
    48→  </Card>
    49→</Cards>
    50→

1→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, uniqueIndex } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     2→import { accounts } from './accounts.js';
     3→
     4→export const dataConnectionStatusEnum = ['active', 'unhealthy', 'disabled'] as const;
     5→export type DataConnectionStatus = (typeof dataConnectionStatusEnum)[number];
     6→
     7→export const dataConnections = pgTable(
     8→  'data_connections',
     9→  {
    10→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(() => `dc_${Date.now()}_${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 10)}`),
    11→    accountId: text('account_id').notNull().references(() => accounts.id, { onDelete: 'cascade' }),
    12→    encryptedConnectionString: text('encrypted_connection_string').notNull(),
    13→    iv: text('iv').notNull(), // Initialization vector for AES-256-GCM
    14→    authTag: text('auth_tag').notNull(), // Authentication tag for AES-256-GCM
    15→    status: text('status', { enum: dataConnectionStatusEnum }).notNull().default('active'),
    16→    lastHealthCheck: timestamp('last_health_check', { withTimezone: true }),
    17→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    18→    updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    19→  },
    20→  (table) => [
    21→    uniqueIndex('idx_data_connections_account').on(table.accountId),
    22→  ]
    23→);
    24→
    25→export type DataConnection = typeof dataConnections.$inferSelect;
    26→export type NewDataConnection = typeof dataConnections.$inferInsert;
    27→

content.content:
1→---
     2→title: Bring Your Own Database (BYOD)
     3→description: Store your audit data in your own PostgreSQL database while using Logseal's API.
     4→---
     5→
     6→BYOD (Bring Your Own Database) lets you store audit events and organizations in your own PostgreSQL database. All other data (accounts, API keys, webhooks, billing) stays in Logseal's infrastructure.
     7→
     8→## Requirements
     9→
    10→- **Business plan** or higher
    11→- A PostgreSQL database (version 14+)
    12→
    13→## How it works
    14→
    15→When BYOD is enabled for your account:
    16→
    17→1. **Audit events** are written to your database instead of Logseal's
    18→2. **Organizations** are created and queried in your database
    19→3. **Everything else** (accounts, API keys, webhooks, billing, schemas, usage) stays in Logseal's database
    20→4. Hash chain integrity, skip-list verification, and Merkle tree proofs all work the same way
    21→
    22→If your database becomes unreachable, Logseal detects this through periodic health checks and marks the connection as `unhealthy`.
    23→
    24→## Setup
    25→
    26→### 1. Create a data connection
    27→
    28→Use the dashboard or API to configure your database connection:
    29→
    30→```bash
    31→curl -X POST https://api.logseal.io/v1/data-connections \
    32→  -H "Cookie: session=YOUR_SESSION_COOKIE" \
    33→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    34→  -d '{"connection_string": "postgresql://user:pass@your-db.example.com:5432/audit_logs"}'
    35→```
    36→
    37→Your connection string is encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM before being stored.
    38→
    39→### 2. Automatic schema initialization
    40→
    41→When you create a connection, Logseal automatically creates the required tables (`organizations` and `audit_events`) in your database. This is idempotent and safe to run multiple times.
    42→
    43→### 3. Verify with a health check
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→curl -X POST https://api.logseal.io/v1/data-connections/health \
    47→  -H "Cookie: session=YOUR_SESSION_COOKIE"
    48→```
    49→
    50→A healthy response confirms your database is reachable and the schema is ready.
    51→
    52→### 4. Start emitting events
    53→
    54→No changes needed to your event emission code. The same `POST /v1/events` endpoint works exactly the same way. Events are automatically routed to your database.
    55→
    56→## What data lives where
    57→
    58→| Data | Location |
    59→|------|----------|
    60→| Audit events | Your database |
    61→| Organizations | Your database |
    62→| Accounts, sessions | Logseal |
    63→| API keys | Logseal |
    64→| Webhooks | Logseal |
    65→| Event schemas | Logseal |
    66→| Usage & billing | Logseal |
    67→| Merkle trees | Logseal |
    68→| Actor profiles (PII) | Logseal |
    69→
    70→## Connection management
    71→
    72→### Disabling a connection
    73→
    74→Set the connection status to `disabled` to temporarily route all queries back to Logseal's database:
    75→
    76→```bash
    77→curl -X PATCH https://api.logseal.io/v1/data-connections \
    78→  -H "Cookie: session=YOUR_SESSION_COOKIE" \
    79→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    80→  -d '{"status": "disabled"}'
    81→```
    82→
    83→### Updating the connection string
    84→
    85→If you need to change your database credentials or host:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→curl -X PATCH https://api.logseal.io/v1/data-connections \
    89→  -H "Cookie: session=YOUR_SESSION_COOKIE" \
    90→  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    91→  -d '{"connection_string": "postgresql://new-user:new-pass@new-host:5432/audit_logs"}'
    92→```
    93→
    94→### Deleting a connection
    95→
    96→Deleting a connection removes the encrypted configuration from Logseal but does **not** delete data in your database:
    97→
    98→```bash
    99→curl -X DELETE https://api.logseal.io/v1/data-connections \
   100→  -H "Cookie: session=YOUR_SESSION_COOKIE"
   101→```
   102→
   103→## Security
   104→
   105→- Connection strings are encrypted using **AES-256-GCM** before storage
   106→- The encryption key is managed by Logseal and never leaves the server
   107→- Data connection endpoints require **session authentication** only (API keys are rejected)
   108→- Health checks run periodically to detect connectivity issues
   109→- Connections that fail 3 consecutive health checks are marked as `unhealthy`
   110→

content.content:
version: '3.8'

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    container_name: traillog-postgres
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: traillog
    ports:
      - '5433:5432'
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./scripts/init-db.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD-SHELL', 'pg_isready -U postgres']
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: traillog-redis
    ports:
      - '6379:6379'
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD', 'redis-cli', 'ping']
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:

content.text:
Excellent! I now have a comprehensive understanding of the LogSeal project. Let me compile a thorough exploration report.

---

# LogSeal Project Exploration Report

## 1. What LogSeal Is - Value Proposition & Problem Solved

**LogSeal** is a **developer-first audit logging API for B2B SaaS companies**. 

### Core Value Proposition:
Instead of building and maintaining your own audit log infrastructure (which typically takes months), companies can integrate LogSeal with a few lines of code and get a production-ready, tamper-proof audit trail for every tenant in their application.

### Problems It Solves:
- **Compliance Requirements**: SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA, and other compliance frameworks require immutable audit trails
- **Customer Trust**: B2B customers demand transparency into what actions are performed in their workspace/tenant
- **Development Time**: Building a robust audit log system from scratch takes months - LogSeal provides it in minutes
- **Tamper-Proof Guarantees**: Cryptographic hash chains make the audit log independently verifiable and tamper-evident
- **Multi-Tenancy Complexity**: Handles organization scoping, test/live environments, and data isolation automatically

### Key Differentiators:
1. **Tamper-Proof Hash Chains**: Every event cryptographically linked to the previous one
2. **Skip-List Verification**: O(log n) hash chain verification using skip-list pointers
3. **Merkle Trees**: Batch events into Merkle trees for cryptographic proofs
4. **BYOD (Bring Your Own Database)**: Business plan feature allowing customers to store audit data in their own PostgreSQL
5. **Embeddable Viewer Tokens**: Short-lived, read-only tokens for customers to view their own audit logs

---

## 2. Tech Stack

### Main Service (Monorepo)
**Location**: `/Users/scottdivelbiss/Dev/logseal`

#### Backend:
- **Language**: TypeScript/Node.js 20+
- **API Framework**: Fastify 4.x with plugins
  - `@fastify/cors` - CORS handling
  - `@fastify/cookie` - Cookie management
  - `@fastify/rate-limit` - Rate limiting
- **Database**: PostgreSQL 16 (via Drizzle ORM 0.45.1)
- **Queue/Cache**: Redis 7 (via ioredis)
- **Job Queue**: BullMQ 5.x for background workers
- **Authentication**: 
  - Argon2 for password hashing
  - JWT for sessions
  - TOTP (otplib) for 2FA
- **Payments**: Stripe (subscription billing)
- **Email**: Resend

#### Frontend:
- **Dashboard**: TanStack Start (SSR) with React 19
  - TanStack Router for routing
  - TanStack Query for data fetching
  - TanStack Table for tables
  - TanStack Form for forms
  - Tailwind CSS 4.x for styling
  - Biome for linting/formatting
  - Vitest for testing
  - Playwright for E2E tests
  - Storybook for component development

- **Landing Page** (`/apps/web`): Astro 5.x with Tailwind CSS 4.x

- **Documentation** (`/apps/docs`): Next.js 16 with Fumadocs
  - Fumadocs UI/Core for MDX-based docs
  - Static generation

#### Infrastructure:
- **Monorepo**: Turborepo 2.x with npm workspaces
- **Build**: TypeScript 5.x, esbuild (via Vite 7.x)
- **Testing**: Vitest 4.x with Testcontainers for integration tests
- **ORM**: Drizzle ORM with drizzle-kit for migrations
- **Containerization**: Docker with multi-stage builds

### SDK Repository
**Location**: `/Users/scottdivelbiss/Dev/logseal-sdks`

#### Node.js SDK (`@logseal/node`):
- **Language**: TypeScript
- **Features**:
  - Automatic event batching
  - Retry logic with exponential backoff
  - Middleware for Express, Fastify, Hono
  - Auto-pagination helpers
  - Graceful shutdown handling

---

## 3. Current State of the Project

### What's Built (Production-Ready):

#### Core Infrastructure ✅
- **API Service** (`/apps/api`): Full RESTful API with 18+ route handlers
- **Worker Service** (`/apps/worker`): 5 worker queues (events, webhooks, retention, merkle, transparency)
- **Dashboard** (`/apps/dashboard`): React 19 SSR application with authentication
- **Documentation Site** (`/apps/docs`): Complete API reference + guides
- **Landing Page** (`/apps/web`): Marketing website with pricing

#### Database Schema ✅
Complete schema with 20+ tables:
- `accounts` - User accounts with 2FA support
- `audit_events` - Core append-only event log with hash chain
- `organizations` - Multi-tenant organizations
- `api_keys` - Test/live API keys
- `event_schemas` - Schema validation
- `webhooks` - Webhook subscriptions
- `exports` - CSV/JSON export jobs
- `merkle_trees` - Merkle tree batch proofs
- `viewer_tokens` - Embeddable viewer tokens
- `data_connections` - BYOD PostgreSQL connections (encrypted)
- `actor_profiles` - PII storage for actors
- `subscriptions` - Stripe billing integration
- `plans` - Free/Pro/Business plans with entitlements

#### Features Implemented ✅
1. **Event Ingestion**
   - Single and batch event emission
   - Hash chain computation with skip-list pointers
   - Idempotency key support
   - Organization auto-creation
   - Schema validation (optional strict mode)

2. **Integrity & Verification**
   - SHA-256 hash chains
   - Skip-list verification (O(log n))
   - Merkle tree construction (Phase 2)
   - Transparency log anchoring (Phase 2)

3. **Authentication & Authorization**
   - Email/password with Argon2
   - Email verification
   - Password reset flow
   - 2FA/TOTP support
   - Session cookies
   - API key authentication (test/live environments)
   - Viewer tokens (read-only, time-limited)
   - Super admin role

4. **Multi-Tenancy**
   - Organizations with test/live separation
   - Default organizations per account
   - External ID mapping
   - Environment isolation

5. **Webhooks**
   - Event subscriptions (`event.created`, `export.completed`, etc.)
   - HMAC-SHA256 signature verification
   - Automatic retries with exponential backoff
   - Webhook delivery tracking

6. **Exports**
   - Async CSV/JSON generation
   - Filtered by date range, action, actor
   - Download URL generation
   - Export job status tracking

7. **BYOD (Bring Your Own Database)**
   - AES-256-GCM encryption of connection strings
   - Automatic schema initialization
   - Health check monitoring
   - Datastore abstraction layer

8. **Billing & Plans**
   - Stripe integration
   - Free/Pro/Business plans
   - Usage tracking per billing period
   - Entitlement enforcement
   - Checkout/portal sessions

#### Testing & CI/CD ✅
- **74 integration tests** in API service covering:
  - Authentication flows
  - Authorization & security (IDOR, mass assignment, rate limiting)
  - Event CRUD operations
  - Webhook delivery
  - Exports
  - API key management
  - Schema management
  
- **CI/CD** (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`):
  - Typecheck job
  - Integration test job (PostgreSQL + Redis services)
  - Migration safety check
  - Schema drift detection (prevents migration/schema desync)

- **Scripts**:
  - `seed.ts` - Comprehensive seed script for local dev
  - Migration safety checker
  - Schema drift checker

#### Deployment ✅
- **Complete deployment guide** (`DEPLOYMENT.md`)
- Dockerfiles for all services:
  - API: `/apps/api/Dockerfile`
  - Worker: `/apps/worker/Dockerfile`
  - Dashboard: `/apps/dashboard/Dockerfile`
  - Docs: `/apps/docs/Dockerfile`
- Coolify deployment on Hetzner VPS
- TLS via Let's Encrypt
- PostgreSQL/Redis managed services

### What's Missing or In Progress:

#### SDK Ecosystem 🔄
- **Node.js SDK**: ✅ Complete in `/logseal-sdks/packages/node`
  - Batching, retry logic, middleware for Express/Fastify/Hono
- **React Component** (`@logseal/react`): 📦 Package exists but implementation unclear
- **Other SDKs**: ❌ Python, Ruby, Go, PHP not started

#### Features Partially Implemented:
1. **Merkle Tree Verification**: Schema + workers exist, but full transparency log not complete
2. **Request Logging**: Schema exists, but usage unclear
3. **Promote (Test→Live)**: Routes exist for preview/execute promotion
4. **Admin Dashboard**: Routes exist but implementation status unclear
5. **Actor Profiles (PII)**: Schema exists for storing actor PII separately

#### Marketing & Documentation 🔄
- **Landing Page**: ✅ Complete with hero, features, pricing, CTA
- **Documentation**: ✅ Extensive guides + API reference
- **Pricing Page**: ✅ Free ($0), Pro ($29), Business ($99)
- **Missing**:
  - Blog/changelog
  - Case studies/testimonials
  - Comparison pages (vs. competitors)
  - SEO optimization unclear

---

## 4. Marketing Materials, Landing Pages, Docs

### Landing Page (`/apps/web`)
**URL**: `http://localhost:4321` (dev), `logseal.io` (prod)

**Sections**:
1. **Hero**: "Audit logging for B2B SaaS, built for developers"
   - Badge: "Now in public beta"
   - CTA: "Get Started" + "View Documentation"
   - Code example showing SDK usage

2. **Features**: 8 feature cards
   - Developer-First API
   - Tamper-Proof Hash Chains
   - Multi-Tenant Organizations
   - Embeddable Viewer Tokens
   - Webhooks with HMAC
   - Async Data Exports
   - Event Schemas
   - SOC2 / Compliance Ready

3. **Code Example Section**: Live TypeScript examples

4. **Integrity Section**: Explanation of hash chain verification

5. **Pricing Cards**: Free/Pro/Business tiers

6. **CTA Footer**: Call-to-action to get started

**Technology**: Astro 5.x with Tailwind CSS 4.x

### Documentation Site (`/apps/docs`)
**URL**: `http://localhost:3002` (dev), `docs.logseal.io` (prod)

**Structure**:
- **Docs**:
  - Getting Started
  - Quickstart
  - Authentication
  - Events
  - Organizations
  - Webhooks
  - Viewer Tokens
  - Exports
  - Hash Verification
  - BYOD (Bring Your Own Database)
  - Error Handling
  - SDK Documentation (Configuration, Batching, Error Handling)

- **API Reference**: Complete OpenAPI-style reference for all endpoints
  - Events API
  - Organizations API
  - API Keys API
  - Webhooks API
  - Schemas API
  - Exports API
  - Viewer Tokens API
  - Merkle Trees API
  - Data Connections API
  - Request Logs API
  - Promote API
  - Billing API
  - Admin API

**Technology**: Next.js 16 with Fumadocs (MDX-based docs framework)

### Pricing
- **Free**: $0/mo - 5,000 events/mo, 30-day retention, 3 orgs, 1 webhook

## Architecture Highlights

### Hash Chain Integrity
Every audit event contains:
- `eventHash` - SHA-256 of previous hash + canonicalized event data
- `previousHash` - Link to previous event in chain
- `sequenceNumber` - Monotonic counter per organization
- `skipHashes` - Skip-list pointers for O(log n) verification

### Skip-List Verification
Events store backward pointers at power-of-2 offsets (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...):
```
Event 100 → points back to Events 99, 98, 96, 92, 84, 68, 36
```
Enables verifying a range of events without scanning every single one.

### Merkle Tree Batching (Phase 2)
- Worker batches events into Merkle trees periodically
- Root hash provides compact proof of all events in batch
- Can anchor root hash to public transparency log

### BYOD Architecture
- `DataStoreManager` maintains pool of connections
- Health checks every 30s (configurable)
- Connection marked `unhealthy` after 3 failed checks
- Events routed to customer DB if active, otherwise Logseal DB
- Only `audit_events` and `organizations` stored in customer DB

### Queue Architecture (BullMQ)
- `audit-events` queue - Event processing (hash chain, store, trigger webhooks)
- `webhooks` queue - Webhook delivery with retries
- `retention` queue - Daily cleanup of expired events
- `merkle-trees` queue - Periodic Merkle tree construction
- `transparency` queue - Anchoring to transparency log

---

## Summary

**LogSeal is a nearly production-ready, feature-complete audit logging platform** with:

✅ **Strengths**:
- Comprehensive backend implementation (API, workers, database)
- Strong cryptographic foundations (hash chains, skip-lists, Merkle trees)
- Excellent developer experience (Node.js SDK, comprehensive docs)
- Modern tech stack (React 19, Fastify, Drizzle, BullMQ)
- Robust testing (74 integration tests, CI/CD with schema drift detection)
- Complete deployment guide (Coolify on Hetzner)
- Well-documented with landing page, docs site, API reference
- Unique BYOD feature for enterprise customers

🔄 **In Progress / Needs Work**:
- React embeddable viewer component (package exists, implementation unclear)
- Additional SDK languages (Python, Ruby, Go, PHP)
- Transparency log anchoring (Phase 2 - workers exist but unclear if complete)
- Marketing content (blog, case studies, testimonials)
- Production deployment evidence (appears to be in development/beta)

❌ **Missing**:
- Multi-language SDK ecosystem
- Public customer examples/testimonials
- Comparison pages vs. competitors
- Blog/changelog

**Overall Assessment**: This is a sophisticated, well-architected SaaS product that demonstrates strong engineering fundamentals. The cryptographic features (hash chains, skip-lists) and BYOD capability are technically advanced differentiators. The codebase is clean, well-tested, and ready for launch or further development.

---

# LogSeal Project Exploration Report

## 1. What LogSeal Is - Value Proposition & Problem Solved

**LogSeal** is a **developer-first audit logging API for B2B SaaS companies**. 

### Core Value Proposition:
Instead of building and maintaining your own audit log infrastructure (which typically takes months), companies can integrate LogSeal with a few lines of code and get a production-ready, tamper-proof audit trail for every tenant in their application.

### Problems It Solves:
- **Compliance Requirements**: SOC2, GDPR, HIPAA, and other compliance frameworks require immutable audit trails
- **Customer Trust**: B2B customers demand transparency into what actions are performed in their workspace/tenant
- **Development Time**: Building a robust audit log system from scratch takes months - LogSeal provides it in minutes
- **Tamper-Proof Guarantees**: Cryptographic hash chains make the audit log independently verifiable and tamper-evident
- **Multi-Tenancy Complexity**: Handles organization scoping, test/live environments, and data isolation automatically

### Key Differentiators:
1. **Tamper-Proof Hash Chains**: Every event cryptographically linked to the previous one
2. **Skip-List Verification**: O(log n) hash chain verification using skip-list pointers
3. **Merkle Trees**: Batch events into Merkle trees for cryptographic proofs
4. **BYOD (Bring Your Own Database)**: Business plan feature allowing customers to store audit data in their own PostgreSQL
5. **Embeddable Viewer Tokens**: Short-lived, read-only tokens for customers to view their own audit logs

---

## 2. Tech Stack

### Main Service (Monorepo)
**Location**: `/Users/scottdivelbiss/Dev/logseal`

- **Language**: TypeScript/Node.js 20+
- **API Framework**: Fastify 4.x with plugins
  - `@fastify/cors` - CORS handling
  - `@fastify/cookie` - Cookie management
  - `@fastify/rate-limit` - Rate limiting
- **Database**: PostgreSQL 16 (via Drizzle ORM 0.45.1)
- **Queue/Cache**: Redis 7 (via ioredis)
- **Job Queue**: BullMQ 5.x for background workers
- **Authentication**: 
  - Argon2 for password hashing
  - JWT for sessions
  - TOTP (otplib) for 2FA
- **Payments**: Stripe (subscription billing)
- **Email**: Resend

#### Frontend:
- **Dashboard**: TanStack Start (SSR) with React 19
  - TanStack Router for routing
  - TanStack Query for data fetching
  - TanStack Table for tables
  - TanStack Form for forms
  - Tailwind CSS 4.x for styling
  - Biome for linting/formatting
  - Vitest for testing
  - Playwright for E2E tests
  - Storybook for component development

- **Landing Page** (`/apps/web`): Astro 5.x with Tailwind CSS 4.x

- **Documentation** (`/apps/docs`): Next.js 16 with Fumadocs
  - Fumadocs UI/Core for MDX-based docs
  - Static generation

#### Infrastructure:
- **Monorepo**: Turborepo 2.x with npm workspaces
- **Build**: TypeScript 5.x, esbuild (via Vite 7.x)
- **Testing**: Vitest 4.x with Testcontainers for integration tests
- **ORM**: Drizzle ORM with drizzle-kit for migrations
- **Containerization**: Docker with multi-stage builds

### SDK Repository
**Location**: `/Users/scottdivelbiss/Dev/logseal-sdks`

- SDK usage examples
- Architecture diagram (Customer App → API → Redis/BullMQ → Worker → PostgreSQL)

**No CLAUDE.md found** in the main repository.

### Key Configuration Files:

#### `/turbo.json`
- Defines build pipeline tasks
- Caching strategy for builds, lint, typecheck, test
- Database tasks marked as `cache: false`

#### `/package.json` (root)
- Monorepo with workspaces: `apps/*`, `packages/*`
- Scripts: `dev`, `build`, `test`, `typecheck`, `lint`, `db:*`
- Dependencies: Testcontainers, Vitest, MSW, Fishery, Prettier, Turbo

#### `/tsconfig.json`
- Base TypeScript configuration for monorepo
- Strict mode enabled

#### `/.env.example`
Comprehensive environment variable template:
- Database/Redis URLs
- API port/host
- Worker concurrency
- JWT secret, TOTP encryption key
- Stripe keys (test/live)
- Resend API key
- Dashboard URL for CORS

#### `/docker-compose.yml`
Local development infrastructure:
- PostgreSQL 16 (port 5433)
- Redis 7 (port 6379)
- Health checks configured
- Persistent volumes

#### `/vitest.workspace.ts`
Vitest workspace configuration for monorepo testing

---

## Architecture Highlights

### Hash Chain Integrity
Every audit event contains:
- `eventHash` - SHA-256 of previous hash + canonicalized event data
- `previousHash` - Link to previous event in chain
- `sequenceNumber` - Monotonic counter per organization
- `skipHashes` - Skip-list pointers for O(log n) verification

### Skip-List Verification
Events store backward pointers at power-of-2 offsets (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...):
```
Event 100 → points back to Events 99, 98, 96, 92, 84, 68, 36
```
Enables verifying a range of events without scanning every single one.

### Merkle Tree Batching (Phase 2)
- Worker batches events into Merkle trees periodically
- Root hash provides compact proof of all events in batch
- Can anchor root hash to public transparency log

### BYOD Architecture
- `DataStoreManager` maintains pool of connections
- Health checks every 30s (configurable)
- Connection marked `unhealthy` after 3 failed checks
- Events routed to customer DB if active, otherwise Logseal DB
- Only `audit_events` and `organizations` stored in customer DB

### Queue Architecture (BullMQ)
- `audit-events` queue - Event processing (hash chain, store, trigger webhooks)
- `webhooks` queue - Webhook delivery with retries
- `retention` queue - Daily cleanup of expired events
- `merkle-trees` queue - Periodic Merkle tree construction
- `transparency` queue - Anchoring to transparency log

---

## Summary

**LogSeal is a nearly production-ready, feature-complete audit logging platform** with:

✅ **Strengths**:
- Comprehensive backend implementation (API, workers, database)
- Strong cryptographic foundations (hash chains, skip-lists, Merkle trees)
- Excellent developer experience (Node.js SDK, comprehensive docs)
- Modern tech stack (React 19, Fastify, Drizzle, BullMQ)
- Robust testing (74 integration tests, CI/CD with schema drift detection)
- Complete deployment guide (Coolify on Hetzner)
- Well-documented with landing page, docs site, API reference
- Unique BYOD feature for enterprise customers

🔄 **In Progress / Needs Work**:
- React embeddable viewer component (package exists, implementation unclear)
- Additional SDK languages (Python, Ruby, Go, PHP)
- Transparency log anchoring (Phase 2 - workers exist but unclear if complete)
- Marketing content (blog, case studies, testimonials)
- Production deployment evidence (appears to be in development/beta)

❌ **Missing**:
- Multi-language SDK ecosystem
- Public customer examples/testimonials
- Comparison pages vs. competitors
- Blog/changelog

**Overall Assessment**: This is a sophisticated, well-architected SaaS product that demonstrates strong engineering fundamentals. The cryptographic features (hash chains, skip-lists) and BYOD capability are technically advanced differentiators. The codebase is clean, well-tested, and ready for launch or further development.

Alright, I've got a thorough picture of where LogSeal stands. Here's my honest breakdown:

---

## Battle Testing Before Launch

You're actually in a **strong position** already (74 integration tests, CI with schema drift detection, security tests for IDOR/mass assignment/rate limiting). But "battle tested" means more than passing tests. Here's what I'd do:

### 1. Stress & Load Testing
- **k6 or Artillery** to hammer the event ingestion endpoint — this is your hot path. You need to know your throughput ceiling on that Hetzner box (events/sec, P99 latency under load).
- Test the hash chain computation under concurrent writes — race conditions here would be catastrophic for integrity.
- Load test BullMQ queue backpressure — what happens when events come in faster than the worker processes them?

### 2. Chaos / Failure Testing
- Kill Redis mid-batch — does the SDK retry gracefully? Do events get lost?
- Kill the worker mid-hash-chain computation — does the chain recover or corrupt?
- BYOD connection drops — does failover to LogSeal's DB actually work?
- Simulate Stripe webhook failures during checkout

### 3. Dogfood It
- **Use LogSeal to audit LogSeal itself.** Instrument your own dashboard — login events, API key creation, settings changes. This is both a battle test AND a marketing story ("we eat our own dogfood").

### 4. Security Audit
- You have security tests, but a quick pass with `npm audit`, dependency scanning, and a manual review of the auth/crypto code paths would be smart before public launch.

---

## Marketing & Hype Strategy

### Immediate Wins (Week 1-2)

**1. Launch on Product Hunt / Hacker News**
- Your tamper-proof hash chain + skip-list verification is genuinely novel. This is the kind of technical depth HN loves.
- Write a "Show HN" post explaining the cryptographic design — not a sales pitch, a technical deep-dive.

**2. Dev.to / Hashnode Blog Posts**
- "Why Your B2B SaaS Needs Audit Logs (and Why You Shouldn't Build Them)"
- "How We Built Tamper-Proof Audit Logs with Hash Chains and Skip Lists"
- "SOC2 Compliance in 5 Minutes: Adding Audit Trails to Your Express App"

**3. Twitter/X + LinkedIn Developer Audience**
- Short code snippets showing how simple the SDK is (3 lines to add audit logging)
- Before/after: "Building audit logs from scratch vs. LogSeal"
- Thread on the cryptographic design decisions

### Medium Term (Month 1-2)

**4. Comparisons & SEO**
- "LogSeal vs. WorkOS Audit Logs" / "LogSeal vs. building your own"
- Target keywords: "audit logging API", "SOC2 audit trail", "B2B SaaS compliance"

**5. Open Source the SDK + React Viewer**
- The SDK is already separate — make it public, polished, with great README
- Open source the `@logseal/react` embeddable viewer — this is a trojan horse for adoption

**6. Free Tier as Growth Engine**
- 5,000 events/mo free is solid. Make signup frictionless — no credit card required.
- Add a "Powered by LogSeal" badge on the free-tier embeddable viewer

**7. Integrations & Templates**
- Next.js + LogSeal starter template
- Express middleware example repo
- Vercel/Railway one-click deploy example

### What Gives You Credibility

**8. Publish Your Integrity Verification**
- Let anyone verify the hash chain via a public endpoint or CLI tool
- This is your **trust moat** — competitors like WorkOS don't have cryptographic proofs

**9. Get 3-5 Beta Users**
- Find indie B2B SaaS founders (Twitter, IndieHackers) who need SOC2 compliance
- Offer them free Pro tier for 6 months in exchange for a testimonial and feedback
- Their real-world usage IS your battle testing

---

## Priority Order

| Priority | Action | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| 1 | Dogfood LogSeal on itself | Battle tests + marketing story |
| 2 | Load test event ingestion | Must know your limits before real users |
| 3 | Get 3-5 beta users | Real traffic > synthetic tests |
| 4 | Write the HN technical deep-dive | Your crypto design is your differentiator |
| 5 | Open source SDKs | Developer trust + adoption flywheel |
| 6 | Polish React viewer + ship it | Embeddable = viral distribution |

---

Want me to start on any of these? I could set up a k6 load test suite, write a dogfooding integration, scaffold blog post outlines, or work on making the SDK repo public-ready. What feels most urgent to you?

input.description: Analyze SDK retry and batch logic
input.prompt:
I need to understand the LogSeal Node.js SDK's client-side behavior for designing failure and load tests. Explore:

1. The batching system - how events are buffered, batch size, flush interval
2. Retry logic - exponential backoff implementation, max retries, what errors trigger retries
3. Graceful shutdown - how pending events are flushed
4. Error handling - what happens when the API is down, returns 429, returns 500
5. The middleware implementations (Express, Fastify, Hono) - what they capture, how they emit
6. Auto-pagination - how it handles large result sets
7. Any connection pooling or keep-alive configuration

Focus on: /Users/scottdivelbiss/Dev/logseal-sdks/packages/node/src

I need to understand exactly what happens client-side when things go wrong - does the SDK lose events? Does it block the application? What's the memory ceiling if events can't be flushed?

I need to understand LogSeal's authentication, authorization, and cryptographic code paths for designing a security audit plan. Explore:

1. Authentication flows - signup, login, session management, JWT handling

3. Password hashing (Argon2) - configuration, parameters
4. 2FA/TOTP implementation - setup, verification, backup codes
5. The hash chain cryptography - SHA-256 implementation, what's included in the hash, canonicalization
6. AES-256-GCM encryption for BYOD connection strings
7. Viewer token generation and validation
8. CORS configuration
9. Rate limiting setup
10. Input validation / sanitization
11. Any SQL injection vectors or unsafe query patterns
12. Cookie security settings (httpOnly, secure, sameSite)
13. HMAC-SHA256 for webhook signatures

content.content:
1→import type { Redis } from 'ioredis';
     2→import { eq, and, gt, sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
     3→import { usageRecords, type Database } from '@logseal/database';
     4→import { invalidateEntitlementsCache } from './entitlements.js';
     5→
     6→export type UsageField =
     7→  | 'eventsCount'
     8→  | 'organizationsCount'
     9→  | 'apiKeysCount'
    10→  | 'webhooksCount'
    11→  | 'exportsCount';
    12→
    13→const COLUMN_MAP: Record<UsageField, string> = {
    14→  eventsCount: 'events_count',
    15→  organizationsCount: 'organizations_count',
    16→  apiKeysCount: 'api_keys_count',
    17→  webhooksCount: 'webhooks_count',
    18→  exportsCount: 'exports_count',
    19→};
    20→
    21→/**
    22→ * Increment a usage counter for an account
    23→ * Uses atomic SQL increment for race condition safety
    24→ */
    25→export async function incrementUsage(
    26→  db: Database,
    27→  redis: Redis,
    28→  accountId: string,
    29→  environment: 'test' | 'live',
    30→  field: UsageField,
    31→  amount: number = 1
    32→): Promise<void> {
    33→  const column = COLUMN_MAP[field];
    34→
    35→  // Get or create current period usage record
    36→  const [existingUsage] = await db
    37→    .select()
    38→    .from(usageRecords)
    39→    .where(and(
    40→      eq(usageRecords.accountId, accountId),
    41→      eq(usageRecords.environment, environment),
    42→      gt(usageRecords.periodEnd, new Date()),
    43→    ))
    44→    .limit(1);
    45→
    46→  if (existingUsage) {
    47→    // Use raw SQL for atomic increment
    48→    await db.execute(
    49→      sql`UPDATE usage_records
    50→          SET ${sql.identifier(column)} = ${sql.identifier(column)} + ${amount},
    51→              updated_at = NOW()
    52→          WHERE id = ${existingUsage.id}`
    53→    );
    54→  } else {
    55→    // Create new usage record for current month
    56→    const now = new Date();
    57→    const periodEnd = new Date(now);
    58→    periodEnd.setMonth(periodEnd.getMonth() + 1);
    59→
    60→    const initialValues: Record<string, number> = {
    61→      eventsCount: 0,
    62→      organizationsCount: 0,
    63→      apiKeysCount: 0,
    64→      webhooksCount: 0,
    65→      exportsCount: 0,
    66→    };
    67→    initialValues[field] = amount;
    68→
    69→    await db.insert(usageRecords).values({
    70→      accountId,
    71→      environment,
    72→      periodStart: now,
    73→      periodEnd: periodEnd,
    74→      ...initialValues,
    75→    });
    76→  }
    77→
    78→  // Invalidate entitlements cache so next check gets fresh usage data
    79→  await invalidateEntitlementsCache(redis, accountId, environment);
    80→}
    81→
    82→/**
    83→ * Decrement a usage counter (e.g., when resource is deleted)
    84→ * Uses atomic SQL decrement with floor at 0
    85→ */
    86→export async function decrementUsage(
    87→  db: Database,
    88→  redis: Redis,
    89→  accountId: string,
    90→  environment: 'test' | 'live',
    91→  field: UsageField,
    92→  amount: number = 1
    93→): Promise<void> {
    94→  const column = COLUMN_MAP[field];
    95→
    96→  const [existingUsage] = await db
    97→    .select()
    98→    .from(usageRecords)
    99→    .where(and(
   100→      eq(usageRecords.accountId, accountId),
   101→      eq(usageRecords.environment, environment),
   102→      gt(usageRecords.periodEnd, new Date()),
   103→    ))
   104→    .limit(1);
   105→
   106→  if (existingUsage) {
   107→    // Use raw SQL for atomic decrement with floor at 0
   108→    await db.execute(
   109→      sql`UPDATE usage_records
   110→          SET ${sql.identifier(column)} = GREATEST(0, ${sql.identifier(column)} - ${amount}),
   111→              updated_at = NOW()
   112→          WHERE id = ${existingUsage.id}`
   113→    );
   114→
   115→    // Invalidate entitlements cache
   116→    await invalidateEntitlementsCache(redis, accountId, environment);
   117→  }
   118→}
   119→
   120→/**
   121→ * Set a usage counter to a specific value
   122→ * Useful for syncing counts after bulk operations
   123→ */
   124→export async function setUsage(
   125→  db: Database,
   126→  redis: Redis,
   127→  accountId: string,
   128→  environment: 'test' | 'live',
   129→  field: UsageField,
   130→  value: number
   131→): Promise<void> {
   132→  const [existingUsage] = await db
   133→    .select()
   134→    .from(usageRecords)
   135→    .where(and(
   136→      eq(usageRecords.accountId, accountId),
   137→      eq(usageRecords.environment, environment),
   138→      gt(usageRecords.periodEnd, new Date()),
   139→    ))
   140→    .limit(1);
   141→
   142→  if (existingUsage) {
   143→    await db
   144→      .update(usageRecords)
   145→      .set({ [field]: value, updatedAt: new Date() })
   146→      .where(eq(usageRecords.id, existingUsage.id));
   147→  } else {
   148→    // Create new usage record
   149→    const now = new Date();
   150→    const periodEnd = new Date(now);
   151→    periodEnd.setMonth(periodEnd.getMonth() + 1);
   152→
   153→    await db.insert(usageRecords).values({
   154→      accountId,
   155→      environment,
   156→      periodStart: now,
   157→      periodEnd: periodEnd,
   158→      [field]: value,
   159→    });
   160→  }
   161→
   162→  // Invalidate entitlements cache
   163→  await invalidateEntitlementsCache(redis, accountId, environment);
   164→}
   165→

1→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, bigint, integer, jsonb, inet, index } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     2→import { createEventId } from '@logseal/utils';
     3→
     4→/**
     5→ * The core audit events table
     6→ * This is append-only - no updates or deletes allowed
     7→ *
     8→ * Note: In production, you'd want to partition this table by received_at
     9→ * for better query performance. Drizzle doesn't support partitioning directly,
    10→ * so you'd create the partition structure via raw SQL migrations.
    11→ */
    12→export const auditEvents = pgTable(
    13→  'audit_events',
    14→  {
    15→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(createEventId),
    16→    accountId: text('account_id').notNull(),
    17→    organizationId: text('organization_id').notNull(),
    18→    environment: text('environment', { enum: ['test', 'live'] }).notNull(),
    19→
    20→    // Event content
    21→    action: text('action').notNull(),
    22→    actorType: text('actor_type').notNull().default('user'),
    23→    actorId: text('actor_id').notNull(),
    24→    actorName: text('actor_name'),
    25→    actorEmail: text('actor_email'),
    26→    actorMetadata: jsonb('actor_metadata').$type<Record<string, unknown>>().default({}),
    27→
    28→    // Targets - array of { type, id, name, metadata }
    29→    targets: jsonb('targets')
    30→      .$type<
    31→        Array<{
    32→          type: string;
    33→          id: string;
    34→          name?: string;
    35→          metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
    36→        }>
    37→      >()
    38→      .notNull()
    39→      .default([]),
    40→
    41→    // Arbitrary metadata
    42→    metadata: jsonb('metadata').$type<Record<string, unknown>>().default({}),
    43→
    44→    // Context
    45→    ipAddress: inet('ip_address'),
    46→    userAgent: text('user_agent'),
    47→    requestId: text('request_id'),
    48→
    49→    // Timing
    50→    occurredAt: timestamp('occurred_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull(),
    51→    receivedAt: timestamp('received_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    52→
    53→    // Integrity - hash chain
    54→    previousHash: text('previous_hash'),
    55→    eventHash: text('event_hash').notNull(),
    56→    sequenceNumber: bigint('sequence_number', { mode: 'number' }).notNull(),
    57→
    58→    // Idempotency
    59→    idempotencyKey: text('idempotency_key'),
    60→
    61→    // Skip-list hash chain (Phase 1)
    62→    skipHashes: jsonb('skip_hashes')
    63→      .$type<Array<{ offset: number; seq: number; hash: string }>>(),
    64→
    65→    // Merkle tree references (Phase 2)
    66→    merkleTreeId: text('merkle_tree_id'),
    67→    merkleLeafIndex: integer('merkle_leaf_index'),
    68→  },
    69→  (table) => [
    70→    // Query indexes
    71→    index('idx_events_org_time').on(table.organizationId, table.occurredAt),
    72→    index('idx_events_org_action').on(table.organizationId, table.action, table.occurredAt),
    73→    index('idx_events_org_actor').on(table.organizationId, table.actorId, table.occurredAt),
    74→    index('idx_events_account_env').on(table.accountId, table.environment, table.receivedAt),
    75→    index('idx_events_idempotency').on(table.accountId, table.idempotencyKey),
    76→    index('idx_events_org_seq').on(table.organizationId, table.sequenceNumber),
    77→  ]
    78→);
    79→
    80→export type AuditEvent = typeof auditEvents.$inferSelect;
    81→export type NewAuditEvent = typeof auditEvents.$inferInsert;
    82→

1→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, boolean, index, uniqueIndex } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     2→import { accounts } from './accounts.js';
     3→import { createOrganizationId } from '@logseal/utils';
     4→
     5→export const organizations = pgTable(
     6→  'organizations',
     7→  {
     8→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(createOrganizationId),
     9→    accountId: text('account_id')
    10→      .notNull()
    11→      .references(() => accounts.id),
    12→    externalId: text('external_id').notNull(),
    13→    name: text('name'),
    14→    environment: text('environment', { enum: ['test', 'live'] }).notNull(),
    15→    isDefault: boolean('is_default').notNull().default(false),
    16→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    17→  },
    18→  (table) => [
    19→    index('idx_orgs_account').on(table.accountId, table.environment),
    20→    uniqueIndex('idx_orgs_unique_per_account').on(
    21→      table.accountId,

4. The worker that processes events - how it handles concurrency, what happens on failure
5. Any rate limiting or backpressure mechanisms

1→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, uniqueIndex } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     2→import { accounts } from './accounts.js';
     3→
     4→export const dataConnectionStatusEnum = ['active', 'unhealthy', 'disabled'] as const;
     5→export type DataConnectionStatus = (typeof dataConnectionStatusEnum)[number];
     6→
     7→export const dataConnections = pgTable(
     8→  'data_connections',
     9→  {
    10→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(() => `dc_${Date.now()}_${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 10)}`),
    11→    accountId: text('account_id').notNull().references(() => accounts.id, { onDelete: 'cascade' }),
    12→    encryptedConnectionString: text('encrypted_connection_string').notNull(),
    13→    iv: text('iv').notNull(), // Initialization vector for AES-256-GCM
    14→    authTag: text('auth_tag').notNull(), // Authentication tag for AES-256-GCM
    15→    status: text('status', { enum: dataConnectionStatusEnum }).notNull().default('active'),
    16→    lastHealthCheck: timestamp('last_health_check', { withTimezone: true }),
    17→    createdAt: timestamp('created_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    18→    updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at', { withTimezone: true }).notNull().defaultNow(),
    19→  },
    20→  (table) => [
    21→    uniqueIndex('idx_data_connections_account').on(table.accountId),
    22→  ]
    23→);
    24→
    25→export type DataConnection = typeof dataConnections.$inferSelect;
    26→export type NewDataConnection = typeof dataConnections.$inferInsert;
    27→

1→import { sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
     2→import { pgTable, text, timestamp, index, uniqueIndex } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
     3→import { accounts } from './accounts.js';
     4→import { createApiKeyId } from '@logseal/utils';
     5→
     6→export const apiKeys = pgTable(
     7→  'api_keys',
     8→  {
     9→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(createApiKeyId),
    10→    accountId: text('account_id')
    11→      .notNull()
    12→      .references(() => accounts.id),
    13→    environment: text('environment', { enum: ['test', 'live'] }).notNull(),
    14→    keyPrefix: text('key_prefix').notNull(),

     4→export const dataConnectionStatusEnum = ['active', 'unhealthy', 'disabled'] as const;

     7→export const dataConnections = pgTable(
     8→  'data_connections',
     9→  {
    10→    id: text('id').primaryKey().$defaultFn(() => `dc_${Date.now()}_${Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 10)}`),

    12→    encryptedConnectionString: text('encrypted_connection_string').notNull(),
    13→    iv: text('iv').notNull(), // Initialization vector for AES-256-GCM
    14→    authTag: text('auth_tag').notNull(), // Authentication tag for AES-256-GCM
    15→    status: text('status', { enum: dataConnectionStatusEnum }).notNull().default('active'),

    22→  ]
